perturbation.original_text: Each Party will comply with any reasonable safety and security measures which may be imposed by the other Party when visiting such other Party's location.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will endeavor to comply with the safety and security suggestions made by the other Party when visiting such other Party's location.
perturbation.explanation: The original text had a clear compliance requirement ('will comply'), whereas the modified text uses the term 'endeavor to comply', which makes the compliance discretionary. It also changes 'measures which may be imposed' to 'suggestions made', further reducing the binding nature of the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This OSHA regulation mandates compliance with safety requirements, whereas the modification makes it discretionary, thus weakening safety obligations. The standard states that “These requirements cover all machines in general industry.” The changed text only implies an optional compliance.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1910.21 - Scope and definitions. | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content Home By Standard Number 1910.21 - Scope and definitions. Part Number: 1910 Part Number Title: Occupational Safety and Health Standards Subpart: 1910 Subpart D Subpart Title: Walking-Working Surfaces Standard Number: 1910.21 Title: Scope and definitions. GPO Source: e-CFR 1910.21(a) Scope . This subpart applies to all general industry workplaces. It covers all walking-working surfaces unless specifically excluded by an individual section of this subpart. 1910.21(b) Definitions . The following definitions apply in this subpart: Alternating tread-type stair means a type of stairway consisting of a series of treads that usually are attached to a center support in an alternating manner such that an employee typically does not have both feet on the same level while using the stairway. Anchorage means a secure point of attachment for equipment such as lifelines, lanyards, deceleration devices, and rope descent systems. Authorized means an employee who the employer assigns to perform a specific type of duty, or allows in a specific location or area. Cage means an enclosure mounted on the side rails of a fixed ladder or fastened to a structure behind the fixed ladder that is designed to surround the climbing space of the ladder. A cage also is called a “cage guard” or “basket guard.” Carrier means the track of a ladder safety system that consists of a flexible cable or rigid rail attached to the fixed ladder or immediately adjacent to it. Combination ladder means a portable ladder that can be used as a stepladder, extension ladder, trestle ladder, or stairway ladder. The components of a combination ladder also may be used separately as a single ladder. Dangerous equipment means equipment, such as vats, tanks, electrical equipment, machinery, equipment or machinery with protruding parts, or other similar units, that, because of their function or form, may harm an employee who falls
perturbation.justification: The original contract language requires each party to 'comply' with safety measures, creating a binding obligation. The changed text weakens this by using 'endeavor to comply' and changes 'measures which may be imposed' to 'suggestions made,' making adherence discretionary. The Law Explanation indicates OSHA regulations mandate compliance with safety requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 2, defines the 'Scope' of OSHA standards in 1910.21(a) as 'all general industry workplaces'. By changing the mandatory 'comply' to the discretionary 'endeavor to comply,' the modified text creates an ambiguity regarding safety obligations, potentially leading to non-compliance within the scope of general industry workplaces, and therefore, there is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall be responsible for its compliance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, including, without limitation, those relating to genetically modified organisms (to the extent RESEARCH PROJECTS involve such organisms).
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall use reasonable efforts to be responsible for compliance with applicable laws, rules and regulations, including, without limitation, those relating to genetically modified organisms (to the extent RESEARCH PROJECTS involve such organisms).
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be responsible for its compliance' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to be responsible for compliance', the obligation is weakened. It is no longer a guaranteed responsibility, but only an effort to be responsible, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: 7 USC § 7711 establishes penalties for violations of the Plant Protection Act. Genetically modified organisms are regulated under this act, to prevent the introduction and spread of plant pests and diseases. Changing “shall be responsible” to “shall use reasonable efforts to be responsible” weakens the requirement for compliance, which could result in a violation of this Act if compliance is not fully achieved. The law dictates firm compliance for containment of plant pests.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 7 / CHAPTER 104 / SUBCHAPTER I / § 7711 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 7 USC 7711 : Regulation of movement of plant pests Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 7-AGRICULTURE CHAPTER 104-PLANT PROTECTION SUBCHAPTER I-PLANT PROTECTION Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous §7711. Regulation of movement of plant pests (a) Prohibition of unauthorized movement of plant pests Except as provided in subsection (c), no person shall import, enter, export, or move in interstate commerce any plant pest, unless the importation, entry, exportation, or movement is authorized under general or specific permit and is in accordance with such regulations as the Secretary may issue to prevent the introduction of plant pests into the Un
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to comply with applicable laws regarding genetically modified organisms by changing 'shall be responsible' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to be responsible'. According to 7 USC § 7711, there are regulations to prevent the introduction of plant pests, and the change weakens the requirement for compliance. The original text mandated responsibility, whereas the changed text only requires 'reasonable efforts,' which could lead to non-compliance and potential violations. The text in Snippet 1, 'no person shall import, enter, export, or move in interstate commerce any plant pest, unless the importation, entry, exportation, or movement is authorized under general or specific permit and is in accordance with such regulations as the Secretary may issue to prevent the introduction of plant pests into the Un,' directly supports the need for strict compliance, which the modified text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party will require any and all of its employees or students who will perform activities in RESEARCH PROJECTS to be bound by a written agreement(s) that commits their inventions, discoveries and other intellectual property to the Party-employer and requires confidential treatment of Party and third-party confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will encourage its employees or students who perform activities in RESEARCH PROJECTS to consider entering into an agreement that addresses their inventions, discoveries and other intellectual property and considers confidential treatment of Party and third-party confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires that employees/students 'be bound by a written agreement', ensuring intellectual property is committed to the employer. The modified version removes this mandatory element by stating that each party will 'encourage its employees or students to consider entering into an agreement' and that they 'consider confidential treatment'. This changes a binding requirement to a mere suggestion, opening the door for disputes over IP ownership and confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: CA Labor Code § 2870 addresses employee invention rights but implicitly expects that agreements regarding IP assignment will be in place. By making IP commitment and confidentiality discretionary, the modification creates a situation where the company's rights to employee inventions are uncertain, and confidentiality is not guaranteed, contradicting the spirit and implied requirements of agreements envisioned by CA Labor Code § 2870. The law assumes some agreement is present, while the modified text makes it optional.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, LAB 2870 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Labor Code - LAB DIVISION 3. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 3.5. Inventions Made by an Employee [2870 - 2872] ( Article 3.5 added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 1001. ) 2870. (a) Any provision in an employment agreement which provides that an employee shall assign, or offer to assign, any of his or her rights in an invention to his or her employer shall not apply to an invention that the employee developed entirely on his or her own time without using the employer’s equipment, supplies, facilities, or trade secret information except for those inventions that either: (1) Relate at the time of conception or reduction to practice of the invention to the employer’s business, or actual or demonstrably anticipated research or development of the employer; or (2) Result from any work performed by the employee for the employer. (b) To the extent a provision in an employment agreement purports to require an employee to assign an invention otherwise excluded from being required to be assigned under subdivision (a), the provision is against the public policy of this state and is unenforceable. (Amended by Stats. 1991, Ch. 647, Sec. 5.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes a mandatory requirement for employees/students to be bound by a written agreement regarding intellectual property and confidentiality to a suggestion that they 'consider' entering into such an agreement. This undermines the implied expectation in CA Labor Code § 2870 that agreements regarding IP assignment will be in place. While the snippet describes inventions made by employees, it is implicitly expected that agreements regarding IP assignment are typically in place. The modified text, by making such agreements optional, contradicts the implied intent of the law. The original text ensures that the employer's rights to employee inventions and confidential information are protected, while the modified text introduces uncertainty and potential disputes, thereby contradicting the law's spirit.
